coredata.dc:title: Ferrocenylseleno-dopamine functionalized carbon dots for redox-gated imaging and drug delivery in cancer cells
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Xiulian
Wang, Xuewen
Li, Aimin
Zhou, Tong
Zhang, Lei
Qu, Jian
Mao, Zhijie
Gu, Ximiao
Zhang, Xin
Jing, Su
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have aroused great interest in the nanomedicine due to their excellent chemical stability and biological compatibility. However, carbon dots-based nanoplatform integrated with targeted cancer imaging and responsive drug delivery are still challenging. Herein, we synthesized strongly green-fluorescent, cancer-cell-targeted and cationic carbon dots (HP-CDs) via hydrothermal method, by using hyaluronic acid (HA) and polyetherimide together as the starting materials. The nanoprobe could be specifically internalized with cancer cells, due to the recognition of surface HA with highly-expressed CD44 receptor. Ferrocenylseleno-dopamine (FcDA) was particularly designed as new generation drug of Fenton-like reagents, assembled on the surface of HP-CDs to obtain CDs@FcDA nanoprobe. In CDs@FcDA nanoprobe, FcDA significantly quenched CDs fluorescence due to photo-induced electron transfer. Upon the cellular specific uptake, ferrocene moiety of the nanoprobe would be oxidized into ferrocenium cation by intracellular H2O2 in a typical Fenton-like reaction, which resulted in charge reversal and drug release from HP-CDs surface. The quenched nanoprobe fluorescence was thus recovered and achieved the cancer-cell-specific imaging. Meantime, the Fenton chemistry produced toxic •OH, leading to the cancer cell apoptosis. Importantly, the composites of CDs@FcDA exhibited lower IC50 value than that of free FcDA for HeLa cell lines, indicating greater therapeutic efficiency of the drug nanoplatform. Moreover, oxidized ferrocenium could react with endogenous glutathione (GSH) and be reversibly restored to the reduced ferrocene, which formed a Fenton-chemistry cycle regulated by H2O2 and GSH in cancer microenvironment. Therefore, the HA-functionalized CDs@FcDA nanoprobe proposed in this work represents an attractive tool for redox-gated imaging and cancer-cell-responsive drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: HA-Derived carbon dots
Ferrocene
Fluorescent probe
Fenton-like chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
Synthesis and characterization of HP-CDs, FcDA and CDs@FcDA
Optical properties of HP-CDs and CDs@FcDA
Cancer-cell targeted uptake and redox-gated imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21671101 , 22175090 and 21861016 ), the Major Basic Research Project of the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions ( 21KJB150019 ) and the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province ( BE2021712 ) and the Jiangsu Key R&D Program ( BE2019708 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a,b) TEM image (a) and hydrodynamic diameter distribution (b) of HP-CDs. (c) FT-IR spectra of HP-CDs. (d) Hydrodynamic diameter distribution of CDs@FcDA. (e,f) Zeta potential (e) and UV–Vis absorption spectra (f) of HP-CDs, FcDA and CDs@FcDA. Data are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD), n = 9.

(a) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of HP-CDs aqueous solution. (b) Dependence of fluorescence emission spectra of HP-CDs on excitation wavelength. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of HP-CDs and CDs@FcDA in absence/presence of H2O2 (3%) at the excitation wavelength of 450 nm. (d) The fluorescence response of DCFH-DA probe to FcDA, FcDA with H2O2 (3%) and CDs@FcDA with H2O2 (3%) at the excitation wavelength of 488 nm.

(a–b) Pictures (a) and the fluorescence signal change (b) of CDs@FcDA probe solution to the sequential addition of H2O2 (3%) and GSH (1 μM) in PBS buffer. (c) The fluorescence responses of CDs@FcDA to various concentrations of H2O2: 0, 0.3%, 0.6%, 1%, 3%, 6% and 10%. (e) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F/F 0 (F was the fluorescence intensity of CDs@FcDA in presence of H2O2, and F 0 was the fluorescence intensity of CDs@FcDA in absence of H2O2) and the logarithmic value of H2O2 concentration. (e) The fluorescence responses of CDs@FcDA + 3% H2O2 to various concentrations of GSH: 0, 0.2, 1.0, 5.0, 7.0, 8.0 and 10 μM. (f) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F 0/F (F was the fluorescence intensity of CDs@FcDA + 3% H2O2 in presence of GSH, and F 0 was the fluorescence intensity of CDs@FcDA + 3% H2O2 in absence of GSH) and the GSH concentrations. The excitation wavelength was 450 nm. Data are presented as the mean ± SD, n = 3.

(a,b) Confocal fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells pre-incubated with HP-CDs probe (a) and CDs@FcDA probe (b) for different times. (c,d) Confocal fluorescence imaging of HUVEC cells pre-incubated with HP-CDs probe (c) and CDs@FcDA probe (d) for different times. Scale bars, 10 μm.

(a–d) CCK-8 assays for the cell viability of HepG2 (a), HUVEC (b), HeLa (c) and AGS (d) cells treated with the probe HP-CDs or CDs@FcDA at different concentrations. (e) Confocal laser scanning microscopic images of MitoROS™ OH580-stained HeLa cells after HP-CDs or CDs@FcDA pre-treatment. MitoROS™ OH580 in PBS was used as a control. Scale bar:10 μm. Data are presented as the mean ± SD (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of HA-functionalized and FcDA-loaded CDs@FcDA nanoprobe and their applications in cancer-cell-specific imaging and redox-gated drug delivery.
IC50 value (μg/mL) of CDs-based probes in different cell lines.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Data are presented as the mean ± SD from the dose−response curves at least three independent experiments.

head.ce:title: Ferrocenylseleno-dopamine functionalized carbon dots for redox-gated imaging and drug delivery in cancer cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have aroused great interest in the nanomedicine due to their excellent chemical stability and biological compatibility. However, carbon dots-based nanoplatform integrated with targeted cancer imaging and responsive drug delivery are still challenging. Herein, we synthesized strongly green-fluorescent, cancer-cell-targeted and cationic carbon dots (HP-CDs) via hydrothermal method, by using hyaluronic acid (HA) and polyetherimide together as the starting materials. The nanoprobe could be specifically internalized with cancer cells, due to the recognition of surface HA with highly-expressed CD44 receptor. Ferrocenylseleno-dopamine (FcDA) was particularly designed as new generation drug of Fenton-like reagents, assembled on the surface of HP-CDs to obtain CDs@FcDA nanoprobe. In CDs@FcDA nanoprobe, FcDA significantly quenched CDs fluorescence due to photo-induced electron transfer. Upon the cellular specific uptake, ferrocene moiety of the nanoprobe would be oxidized into ferrocenium cation by intracellular H2O2 in a typical Fenton-like reaction, which resulted in charge reversal and drug release from HP-CDs surface. The quenched nanoprobe fluorescence was thus recovered and achieved the cancer-cell-specific imaging. Meantime, the Fenton chemistry produced toxic •OH, leading to the cancer cell apoptosis. Importantly, the composites of CDs@FcDA exhibited lower IC50 value than that of free FcDA for HeLa cell lines, indicating greater therapeutic efficiency of the drug nanoplatform. Moreover, oxidized ferrocenium could react with endogenous glutathione (GSH) and be reversibly restored to the reduced ferrocene, which formed a Fenton-chemistry cycle regulated by H2O2 and GSH in cancer microenvironment. Therefore, the HA-functionalized CDs@FcDA nanoprobe proposed in this work represents an attractive tool for redox-gated imaging and cancer-cell-responsive drug delivery.

A self-targeted and redox-gated nanoprobe was designed by using ferrocenylseleno-functionalized fluorescent carbon dots to achieve cancer-cell responsive theranostics.
A self-targeted and redox-gated nanoprobe was designed by using FcDA-functionalized fluorescent carbon dots to achieve cancer-cell responsive theranostics.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have unique particle size, good biocompatibility and low toxicity, leading to widely exploration in biological imaging and disease theranostic [1–5]. Carbon sources as well as synthetic methods are crucial to their chemical properties and subsequent biomedical application [6–9]. Polymer precursors with targeted specificity are particularly attractive carbon sources, which can be used to prepare CDs with a self-targeted function [10–12]. A convictive example is to use biocompatible hyaluronic acid (HA), a water-soluble acidic polysaccharide. HA has strong interactions with the cancer-cell biomarker of cluster determinant 44 (CD44) receptors on cell membranes [13–17]. Thus HA-initiated CDs have great potential in targeted bioimaging and imaging-guided therapy [12]. For example, a self-targeted fluorescent carbon dot was synthesized via HA carbonization in a dehydrating agent at controlled temperature, which was further used for cancerous MDAMB-231 imaging [10]. Combining photosensitizing activity of HA-derived carbon dots, Shen group achieved self-targeted and imaging-guided photodynamic therapy of cancer cells [12]. Despite these achievements, the luminescent-keeping-on HA-CDs lack of smart response and thus may lead to false diagnostic signal, which can be problematic for application in living cancer cells. There is an urgent need to develop responsive turn-on fluorescent CDs for target activation imaging and controllable drug delivery.

Organometallic ferrocene (Fc) contains ferrous iron between two cyclopentadienyl rings, which shows good chemical stimulus capability through a one-electron redox reaction. The states between Fc state and oxidized ferrocenium state (Fc+) are reversible [18–22]. Moreover, the redox between Fc and Fc+ states can switch “off/on” the fluorescence of conjugated organic fluorophores via photo-induced electron transfer (PET) pathway [23–26]. By virtue of the PET effect, ferrocene-contained fluorescent probes have been developed for the target detection [27]. For example, Fenton-like green chemistry allows the hydrophobic Fc moieties oxidized into hydrophilic Fc+ ones, which can be used as a charge-responsive switch for in vivo sensing of H2O2 [28]. Fenton-like reaction, in which H2O2 is catalyzed by Fc moieties to produce cytotoxic •OH for oxidative stress amplification, is an emerging cancer treatment approach of chemodynamic therapy (CDT) [29–39]. For example, Fc was incorporated into a tumor-targeting peptide ligand to enable targeting integrin subtypes over-expressed in various tumors, which demonstrated Fc-mediated ROS production and therapeutic activity [40]. Several Fc-loaded nanocarrier systems were constructed for CDT via the Fenton-like reaction, including block copolymers [30,33], supramolecular pillar[n]arenes [32] and mesoporous silica nanoparticles [29,41], to name only a few. Our group developed Fc-loaded upconversion nanoparticles to achieve upconversion luminescence (UCL) imaging-guided photo-CDT via NIR-triggered Fenton reaction [41,42]. For further theranostic applications of Fenton reaction-based CDT approach, biosafe nanocarrier with imaging function is still the objective of the exploration.

In this work, we prepared a biocompatible, self-targeting and green-fluorescent CDs, integration of ferrocene/ferrocenium redox-gated switch, to achieve cancer-cell-specific imaging and drug delivery (Scheme 1 ). Water-soluble fluorescent carbon dots HA-PEI CDs (HP-CDs) were synthesized in one step by hydrothermal method using HA and PEI as carbon precursors. The resulting cationic carbon dots were then used as the nanocarrier for the anticancer Fenton reagents of ferrocenylseleno-dopamine (FcDA) reported previously by our group [35], to form CDs@FcDA nanoprobe. CDs@FcDA nanoprobe was characterized by TEM, FT-IR, DLS and Zeta potential measurements. The HA residues on the probe surface offer good targeting for over-expressed CD44 receptor on membranes of cancer cells; meanwhile, due to the PET effect of ferrocene, the fluorescence of HP-CDs is strongly quenched. Upon specific uptake of cancer cells through CD44 receptor, Fc is oxidized to Fc + upon stimulation by intracellular high concentrations of H2O2, thus the positively-charged drug was repulsed from cationic HP-CDs surface, which results in the recovery of probe fluorescence for enabling fluorescence imaging of cancer cells. The released Fenton drug could be recycled by intracellular redox homeostasis of GSH/H2O2. Our self-targeted fluorescent HP-CDs delivery system can be used as a promising therapeutic platform for redox-gated fluorescence imaging and chemodynamic therapy of cancers.

All starting chemicals were obtained commercially without further purification. Hyaluronic acid (HA) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) was obtained from Yuanye Bio-Technology Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Penicillin, streptomycin and fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd. Human gastric adenocarcinoma (AGS), human hepatoma (HepG2), human cervical adenocarcinoma (HeLa) and Human umbilical vein endothelial (HUVEC) cell lines were purchased from KeyGen Biotechnology (Nanjing, China). Cellular hydroxyl radical detection kit (red-fluorescence) was purchased from AAT Bioquest Inc. (USA). CCK-8 kit was obtained from Dojindo Laboratories (Japan). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.4) buffer contained 136.7 mM NaCl, 2.7 mM KCl, 8.72 mM Na2HPO4 and 1.41 mM KH2PO4. All other reagents were of analytical grade. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (≥18 MΩ, Milli-Q, Millipore).

The Zeta potentials and hydrodynamic diameters of HP-CDs and CDs@FcDA were determined on the instrument of Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images of HP-CDs and CDs@FcDA were carried out with a JEM-1400 (JEOL) operated at 100 kV. Absorption spectra were measured using an ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (LAMBDA950, PerkinElmer, Germany). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements were performed using a Nicolet iS50 spectrometer. Fluorescent spectra were recorded on an Edinburgh FLS1000 luminescence spectrometer. Live cell imaging was conducted on a Zeiss LSM880 NLO confocal laser scanning microscopy.

Ferrocenylseleno-dopamine (FcDA) was prepared according to a reported method from our previous paper [35]. The synthetic route of FcDA was shown in Scheme 2 . The product was successfully characterized by 1H NMR, 13C NMR and high-resolution Mass Spectrometry (Figs. S1–3). 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ 8.79 (s, 1H), 8.68 (s, 1H), 8.04 (t, J = 5.6 Hz, 1H), 6.64 (s, 1H), 6.59 (d, J = 2.0 Hz, 1H), 6.45 (dd, J = 7.9, 2.0 Hz, 1H), 4.33 (s, 4H), 4.29 (d, J = 1.9 Hz, 2H), 4.27 (d, J = 1.9 Hz, 2H), 3.47 (s, 2H), 3.44 (s, 2H), 3.18 (s, 2H), 2.94 (d, J = 3.6 Hz, 1H), 2.51 (s, 2H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) δ 172.0, 144.0, 130.6, 119.7, 116.5, 115.9, 71.09, 68.95, 68.62, 47.77, 39.97. HRMS [M + H+] calculated for C22H23FeNO3Se2 565.9358 u, found 565.9446 u.

HP-CDs were prepared by a hydrothermal method using hyaluronic acid (HA) and polyethyleneimine (PEI) as raw materials. Briefly, HA and PEI (m:m = 1:1) were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water, stirred for 10 min and then the mixture was transferred to a 15 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) vessel and heated at 180 °C for 4 h. The reaction vessel was then slowly cooled to room temperature and the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to get the supernatant. The resulting supernatant was dialyzed in distilled water for 48 h using a 3500 Da dialysis bag to remove unreacted staring materials (the dialysate was changed every 12 h). HP-CDs were obtained by freezing and then stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C prior to usage.

The obtained HP-CDs and FcDA were dissolved in ultrapure water and ethanol, respectively, at the mass ratio of 1:3. The mixture was stirred for 24 h and subsequently dialyzed using ethanol for another 24 h to remove unbound FcDA. CDs@FcDA were finally obtained by freezing and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C prior to usage.

The amount of FcDA loaded on HP-CDs was determined by UV–vis absorption spectrum measurements of FcDA and HP-CDs at 464 nm and 347 nm, respectively. The absorption of the supernatant, containing free FcDA removed from the particle, was converted to concentration of the corresponding FcDA with a standard linear calibration curve. The standard curve was obtained with known concentrations of FcDA. Finally, the loading efficiency of FcDA per particle was obtained by dividing the subtracted weight of FcDA by the total weight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) can be calculated using the following equation [43]:
QY =
∫ Lemission
∫ Esolvent − ∫ Esample
Where QY is the quantum yield, L emission is the photon numbers of the FL emission of HP-CDs, CDs@FcDA. E sample and E solvent are the photon numbers of the excitation light used for sample and the solvent (PBS buffer), respectively. The excitation wavelength was used is 450 nm.

CDs@FcDA dissolved in 1 mL of PBS buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.0) was incubated with 1 mL of 3% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) at 37 °C for 30 min, and the fluorescence spectrum was measured. The solution was then stirred well and an excess of GSH was added. After a 30-min stirring, the fluorescence spectra of the reaction solution were examined again. The fluorescence spectra were collected from 460 to 700 nm at the excitation of 450 nm.

Human gastric adenocarcinoma (AGS), human hepatoma cell line (HepG2), human cervical adenocarcinoma (HeLa) and human umbilical vein endothelial (HUVEC) cell lines were individually cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Gibco) supplemented with 10% fetal calf serum (FCS, Gibco), penicillin (100 μg/mL) and streptomycin (100 μg/mL) at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. Cell number was determined using a Petroff-Hausser cell counter (USA).

HeLa cells were seeded into 35-mm confocal dishes (5 × 103 per dish) and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. The well-grown HeLa cells were incubation with CDs@FcDA nanoprobe (3.12 μg/mL, 1 mL) for 4 h at 37 °C and washed with PBS three times. The treated cells were further incubated with MitoROS™ OH probe for 40 min at 37 °C. The cells were washed with PBS and finally visualized by confocal scanning fluorescence microscopy with a laser source set at 488 nm and recording at 500–550 nm.

The cytotoxicity of HP-CDs and CDs@FcDA probes were examined by Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8). Both cancer and normal cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 per well (100 μL) and then incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. The cultured cells were treated with HP-CDs or CDs@FcDA (100 μL) at different concentrations starting from 200 μg/mL. The mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for another 72 h. After washed with clean PBS, the cells in each well were added with CCK-8 solution (5%) and treated for 2 h at 37 °C. Finally, the absorbance of each well was measured at a wavelength of 450 nm with a microplate reader. The relative cell viability (%) was calculated by (A test/A control) × 100%.

HeLa and HUVEC cells were seeded into 35-mm confocal dishes (Glass Bottom Dish) at a density of 5.0 × 103 per dish and grown to 70–80% confluence. Cells were rinsed in Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline and kept in PBS during the imaging procedure. Subsequently, the cell treatments can be divided into three groups. The first group contains the cells incubated with clean PBS for 4 h at 37 °C. In the second group, cells were incubated with HP-CDs probe (3.12 μg/mL, 1 mL) for 4 h at 37 °C. In the third group, HeLa cells were incubated with CDs@FcDA probe (3.12 μg/mL, 1 mL) for 4 h at 37 °C. Cancer cells and HUVEC normal cells were conducted in parallel. All the cells were finally washed with PBS before imaging. The cells were excited at 450 nm and the emission was collected from 500 to 600 nm. All fluorescence images of the living cells were obtained on a Zeiss confocal scanning fluorescence microscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of HP-CDs, FcDA and CDs@FcDA
ce:section.ce:para: HA and PEI were used as the carbon precursors to synthesize HP-CDs via hydrothermal method in one pot at 180 °C for 4 h as shown in Scheme 1. After size selection with a centrifugation method, the morphology of the synthesized HP-CDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with average diameter 22.69 ± 4.2 nm (Fig. 1 a and Fig. S4). Their hydrodynamic diameter size was close to 69.44 nm obtained from the dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis (Fig. 1b). The broad size distribution suggested HP-CDs tends to aggregate in aqueous solution. The starting materials of HA and PEI were polymers, and their monomer unites or residues can be retained on the surface of the as-prepared HP-CDs [12,44], which was demonstrated by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum (Fig. 1c). The peaks at 3427 cm−1, 2901 cm−1, 1633 cm−1, 1407 cm−1 and 1057 cm−1 in the spectrum were attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H or N–H, C–H, CO, C–N and C–O–C of HA (Fig. 1c and Fig. S5), respectively, and a broad band centered around 3427 cm−1 suggested the existence of –NH2 group of PEI [12,45]. The strongly positive-charged PEI residues were beneficial for electrostatic binding of negative-charged materials, meanwhile HA residues could improve their biocompatibility [12].

Surface functionalization of HP-CDs with FcDA was sequentially characterized through DLS, TEM, Zeta potential and UV–Vis absorption measurements (Fig. 1d–f and Figs. S6–10). The evenly-distributed hydrodynamic diameter of CD@FcDA suggested the size of nanocomposites were uniform (Fig. 1d), which showed the average diameter of 40.81 ± 9.41 nm evaluated from the TEM images (Figs. S6–7). The DLS analysis indicated the CDs@FcDA improved the homogeneous aqueous dispersion of HP-CDs, which was beneficial to intracellular assays. The electrostatic attraction between positively charged HP-CDs (ζ 18.6 ± 2.61 mV) and negative FcDA (ζ −8.15 ± 3.44 mV) triggered the formation of CDs@FcDA (ζ 10.0 ± 0.29 mV) nanocomposite (Figs. S8–S10). The surface of CDs@FcDA nanoprobe showed a more negative potential after modified with electronegative FcDA (Fig. 1e), indicating better dispersibility of the nanoprobe in aqueous solution. In the UV–Vis absorption spectra (Fig. 1f), a broad and distinct band at around 347 nm was attributed to the π-π* transition of CC from the prepared HP-CDs. After FcDA loading, a new characteristic absorption at around 464 nm was observed, which was assigned to FcDA molecule. By measuring the absorbance at 464 nm, a loading efficiency of FcDA on the HP-CDs was estimated to be 17.53% (Figs. S11–12). The CDs@FcDA nanoprobe could keep desirable stability for 72 h in PBS, which was favorable for the internalization into cancer cells via CD44 receptor-mediated endocytosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of HP-CDs and CDs@FcDA
ce:section.ce:para: After the structure characterizations, their optical properties were evaluated by fluorescent spectra measurements. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the HP-CDs showed fluorescent peak at 533 nm when the maximum excitation wavelength was fixed at 450 nm, and their excitation and emission spectra are mirror symmetry. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was measured as 4.69%, which was comparable to those previously-reported CDs, including 5% of boron- and nitrogen-co-doped CDs by a top-down method [46] and 2.83%–6.81% of nitrogen-doped CDs via microwave-assisted hydrothermal method [47]. Interestingly, the fluorescent emission spectrum of HP-CDs is dependent on the excitation wavelength. As the excitation wavelength increased from 300 nm to 500 nm, the corresponding emission peak exhibited gradual red-shifts (Fig. 2b), which is in well accordance with typical fluorescent CDs [48,49]. Although the wavelength-dependent fluorescence properties of CDs have not been clearly clarified, the majority of explanations were attributed to the synergistic effect of carbogenic core and molecular surface state of the CDs [50]. After FcDA assembly through electrostatic adsorption, the fluorescence of CDs@FcDA (QY = 4.38%) was significantly quenched (Fig. 2c), due to PET effect of ferrocene derivatives [51]. Upon the addition of H2O2, FcDA was involved in a Fenton-like reaction in which Fc moiety was oxidized to Fc+ one [52,53], and thus the fluorescence of CDs@FcDA was restored due to the PET inhibition [24]. At the same time, the FcDA precipitation was observed along with the occurrence of Fenton-like reaction in presence of H2O2 (Fig. 3 a and b), which was attributed to that the oxidized FcDA repelled cationic HP-CDs and caused the dissociation of CDs@FcDA nanocomposite. Fig. 3c showed the fluorescence spectra of CDs@FcDA as functions of H2O2 concentrations from 0.3% to 10%. The increasing H2O2 concentration obviously led to the gradual fluorescence recovery. There was a good linear relationship between F/F 0 and the logarithmic value of H2O2

Concentration (Fig. 3d), whereas F was the fluorescence intensity of CDs@FcDA in presence of H2O2, and F 0 was the fluorescence intensity of CDs@FcDA in absence of H2O2. The detection limit of H2O2 was estimated to be 6.99 × 10−5 mol L−1 at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (3σ). It is well-accepted that the redox couple Fc/Fc+ is reversible under the regulation of H2O2/GSH [54–56]. Therefore, the further addition of GSH leaded to the precipitate disappearance and subsequent occurrence of transparent solution (Fig. 3a), suggesting the reduction of Fc+ to Fc by GSH could trigger the electrostatic adsorption of FcDA onto the surface of HP-CDs again. The plot derived from the decreased fluorescence intensity vs. GSH concentration fitted a linear in the concentration range of 0.20–10.0 μmol L−1 (Fig. 3e and f). The detection limit at 3σ was 1.57 × 10−7 mol L−1 for GSH. The data displayed in Fig. 3 indicates that the fluorescence property of the CDs probe was tunable via the FcDA-based Fenton-like reaction, which could be used as the redox-responsive imaging probe in living cells.

FcDA-based Fenton-like reaction resulted in toxic •OH generation, which has been indicated the anti-tumor activity [35]. To evaluate this, ROS-specific fluorescence probe of DCFH-DA (2,7-dichlorodi-hydrofluorescein diacetate) [57] was used to demonstration the production of •OH via Fenton chemistry. As shown in Fig. 2d, either the probe alone or its incubation with FcDA showed weak fluorescence. However, in presence of H2O2, the fluorescence of ROS probe was obviously increased at 520 nm upon the incubation with FcDA. Similarly, the CDs@FcDA nanocomposite also induced the fluorescence enhancement of DCFH-DA probe. These results verified the efficient production of •OH through FcDA-based Fenton-like reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cancer-cell targeted uptake and redox-gated imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of HA-mediated intracellular endocytosis of CDs@FcDA by cancer cells, confocal fluorescence imaging of living cancer cells incubated with the nanoprobe was conducted. To address this, HeLa cancer cells and HUVEC normal cells were performed by incubating with different probes at 37 °C. As shown in Fig. 4 a and b, the fluorescence from HP-CDs-treated HeLa cells was lighted up at 4 h, which was more prominent than those incubated with CDs@FcDA, suggesting that the carbon dot fluorescence was quenched initially by the loaded FcDA. After an 8-h incubation, the fluorescence image of CDs@FcDA-treated cells was brighter and.

The cell morphology tended to undergo apoptosis. However, HeLa cells incubated with HP-CDs in absence of FcDA kept stable fluorescence imaging and well morphology when compared with them under the 4-h incubation condition. Higher brightness of fluorescence imaging was observed for HeLa cells incubated with HP-CDs or CDs@FcDA at the increased concentration for 4 h (Fig. S13). The confocal living cell imaging suggested the fluorescence of internalized CDs was further enhanced upon the Fenton-like oxidization of loaded FcDA by intracellular H2O2. From the appearance of bright-field images, the increasing degree of cell apoptosis was obvious along with the incubation time of CDs@FcDA. In contrast, identically treated HUVEC cells did not give appreciable change in fluorescence imaging and cellular morphology (Fig. 4c–d and Fig. S14). Therefore, CDs@FcDA can be used as a cancer-cell-specific and redox-gated imaging probe, offering precise diagnostics for the subsequent therapy.

The chemodynamic efficiency of the nanoprobe against cancer cells were evaluated via cell proliferation analysis. At each probe concentration, the cytotoxicity of the correspondingly treated cells was examined using a standard CCK-8 assay (Fig. 5 a–d). HepG2, AGS and HeLa cancer cells and HUVEC normal cells were seeded at the density of 5 × 103 cells/well in 96-well plates and cultured overnight. Different concentrations of HP-CDs/CDs@FcDA probe were added in three replicates and cultured for 72 h. Before FcDA loading, the HP-CDs were little cytotoxic. 72-h incubation with the CDs@FcDA nanoprobe among the concentrations of 1.56–200 μg/mL could maintain HepG2 and HUVEC cells more than 95% cell viability (Fig. 5a and b), demonstrating low toxicity of HP-CDs alone. To some extent, HP-CDs-incubated HeLa cells and AGS cells were less biocompatible when compared with HepG2 and HUVEC cells (Fig. 5c and d), which could be attributed to that the higher expression of CD44 receptor mediated more probe uptaken [58–60]. With the assembly of FcDA, the resulting CDs@FcDA nanoprobe exhibited remarkable cytotoxicity to all tested cancer cells, however, they did not exhibit significant toxic effect on normal cells. Among the studied cancer cells, CDs@FcDA exhibited the lowest IC50 value of 3.94 μg/mL for HeLa cells, the value of which was 2.7 times lower than that of FcDA alone, indicating that the self-targeted CDs-based nanocomposite exhibited a better therapeutic activity. It should be noted that the anticancer activity was mostly caused by the FcDA-based Fenton therapy, however, dopamine moiety of FcDA could react with intracellular H2O2, the product of which would inhibit the thioredoxin reductases and also attributed to the cytotoxicity of the cancer cells [61]. All the IC50 results were summarized in Table 1 .

To confirm intracellular ROS-caused chemodynamic therapy through the Fenton-like reaction, the nanoprobe-treated HeLa cells were stained with a hydroxyl radical-responsive probe (MitoROS™ OH580), which was turned to be a highly fluorescent product upon specific reaction with •OH. As shown in Fig. 5e, HeLa cells incubated with MitoROS™ OH580 in PBS exhibited negligible red fluorescence, whereas, a dramatic increase in the fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells was observed on confocal laser scanning microscopy when pre-treated with CDs@FcDA. In the meantime, the HP-CDs-incubated cells did not significantly light up the red fluorescence of MitoROS™ OH580. These results indicated that a considerable amount of •OH was produced upon the treatment of cancer cells by CDs@FcDA nanoprobe, which is consistent with the demonstrations in vitro shown in Fig. 2d. The whole Fig. 5 revealed that CDs@FcDA nanoprobe could provide a responsive imaging-guided platform for chemodynamic therapy against cancer cells.

In the current study, HA- and ferrocenylseleno-functionalized fluorescent carbon dots, CDs@FcDA, have been presented for redox-gated cancer cell imaging and drug delivery. The cationic CDs were hydrothermally prepared using HA and PEI in one step and then loaded with anti-cancer Fenton-like reagents of FcDA through electrostatic interaction. The CDs@FcDA nanoprobe possesses quenched fluorescence via PET and well stability in average physiological conditions. After HA-mediated uptake, the nanoprobe can be efficiently internalized by cancer cells. The loaded FcDA was subsequently oxidized into Fc+ state via Fenton-like reaction, the resulting charge reversal made FcDA away from carbon dot surface and thus recovered its fluorescence. Meanwhile, the generation of •OH induced cell apoptosis. CDs@FcDA exhibited the IC50 value as low as 3.94 μg/mL for the HeLa cells, which is more potent than FcDA independent on CDs. The therapeutic activity is notable in different cancer cells but not in normal cells. The unique ferrocenylseleno functionalization endows the bio-friendly carbon dots with new insight into cancer chemodynamic therapy.

Xiulian Lu, Lei Zhang and Su Jing planned the projects, designed the experiments and wrote the manuscript. Xiulian Lu and Xuewen Wang performed the experiments. Aimin Li, Tong Zhou, Jian Qu, Zhijie Mao, Ximiao Gu and Xin Zhang gave assistance. Lei Zhang and Su Jing supervised and coordinated all investigators for this project. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would gratefully acknowledge financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21671101, 22175090 and 21861016), the Major Basic Research Project of the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (21KJB150019) and the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2021712) and the Jiangsu Key R&D Program (BE2019708).
